Spanning over 20,000 acres with 500 miles of shoreline, this massive reservoir offers incredible opportunities for anglers — but only if you know where to look. That's where an experienced SML striper guide makes all the difference between a frustrating day of searching and a memorable day of catching.

title: Best Time to Fish Smith Mountain Lake | Striper Seasons
metaDescription: Discover the best time to fish Smith Mountain Lake for stripers. Seasonal fishing guide covering spring, summer, fall & winter striper patterns in Virginia.
content:
Best Time to Fish Smith Mountain Lake for Striped Bass

One of the most common questions Captain Tommy Moore gets is: "What's the best time to fish Smith Mountain Lake?" The honest answer is that Smith Mountain Lake offers excellent striper fishing year-round — but understanding the striper fishing seasons helps you pick the ideal window for your trip and set the right expectations for what you'll encounter on the water.

Located in Virginia's Blue Ridge Mountains near Moneta, VA, Smith Mountain Lake benefits from a temperate climate that keeps striped bass active in every season. The lake's deep, well-oxygenated water and abundant forage base sustain healthy striper populations through even the hottest summers and coldest winters. Here's your complete seasonal breakdown from SML Wicked Striper Guide Service.

Spring Striper Fishing (March – May)

Spring is arguably the most exciting time to fish Smith Mountain Lake. As water temperatures climb through the 50s and into the 60s, stripers move from deep winter haunts toward the upper reaches of the lake and tributary arms. This is the pre-spawn and spawn period, and fish are aggressive, hungry, and often found in shallower water than any other time of year.

Key spring patterns include:

  • Stripers chasing shad in creek channels and flats
  • Surface feeding activity during low-light periods, especially at dawn
  • Fish staging on main-lake points before moving up tributaries
  • Excellent action on both live bait and artificial lures
  • Concentrations of big pre-spawn females in predictable staging areas

Captain Tommy's spring trips frequently produce multiple fish over 20 pounds, with shots at true trophies in the 30- to 40-pound class. The transition from winter to spring patterns usually begins in mid-March and peaks through April, making this window one of the most productive periods on the calendar.

Spring weather in the Blue Ridge Mountains can be variable — cool mornings, warm afternoons, and occasional rain showers are common. Dressing in layers is essential. But a little weather often makes the fishing even better, as overcast skies extend the morning bite and keep fish active longer.

Summer Striper Fishing (June – August)

Summer on Smith Mountain Lake means warm surface temperatures, a well-established thermocline, and stripers that settle into predictable deep-water patterns. Fish stack up in the thermocline zone, typically 25 to 45 feet deep, where water temperatures and dissolved oxygen levels are ideal for their metabolism.

This is prime time for downlining live bait — Captain Tommy's bread and butter technique. Using fresh gizzard shad presented at precise depths, you'll target schools of stripers holding over main-lake humps, channel bends, and deep points. The fish are predictable, the bites are consistent, and the technique is accessible to anglers of all skill levels.

Summer also brings reliable early-morning topwater blitzes that will get your heart pounding. Before the sun climbs high and recreational boat traffic picks up, stripers push bait to the surface in explosive feeding frenzies. Getting on the water early is key — Captain Tommy times departures to coincide with these pre-dawn and dawn windows.

Summer is also the most popular time for family fishing trips, as school schedules align and vacation time opens up. The warm weather makes for comfortable days on the water, and the consistent bite means kids stay engaged and excited.

Fall Striper Fishing (September – November)

Fall is a sleeper season that many anglers overlook, and that's a mistake. As the lake turns over and water temperatures equalize, stripers go on a feeding binge to pack on weight for winter. Bait schools get pushed into coves and pockets, and hungry stripers follow them in — often with reckless abandon.

Fall fishing on Smith Mountain Lake often produces the highest catch numbers of the year. Fish are scattered across the water column, which means multiple techniques come into play — from trolling umbrella rigs to casting topwater plugs to vertical jigging over deep structure. Captain Tommy adjusts daily based on conditions, and some of his most varied and exciting trips happen during the fall transition.

The scenery during fall is an added bonus. The Blue Ridge Mountains surrounding Smith Mountain Lake put on a stunning display of autumn colors, making fall trips a visual feast even between bites.

Winter Striper Fishing (December – February)

Don't put the rods away when it gets cold. Winter striper fishing at Smith Mountain Lake can be phenomenal for anglers willing to brave the elements. Cold water concentrates fish into tight schools near deep structure, and when you find them, the action can be fast and furious. Winter fish are also some of the fattest of the year, heavy with eggs and stored energy — this is when the biggest fish of the season are caught.

Captain Tommy uses his Humminbird electronics to locate deep schools, then presents live bait right in their wheelhouse. Water clarity is typically excellent in winter, and there's almost zero boat traffic — it's just you, the fish, and the mountains. The solitude and quality of winter fishing make it a favorite season for many of Captain Tommy's repeat clients.

Winter trips do require proper cold-weather clothing. Thermal layers, waterproof outerwear, gloves, and warm hats are essential. But the reward for dressing properly is outstanding fishing with virtually no competition on the water.

Book the Right Season for Your Goals

Want trophy-class fish? Book spring or winter. Want nonstop action and high catch numbers? Fall is your season. Want a beautiful day on the water with consistent catching and comfortable weather? Summer delivers every time. No matter when you come, Captain Tommy Moore will put you in the best position to succeed.

Book your Smith Mountain Lake fishing trip today or call Captain Tommy at (434) 610-3255 to discuss the best dates for your goals. Check out our trip options and pricing to find the right fit.

title: Striped Bass Fishing Techniques | How to Catch Stripers
metaDescription: Master striped bass fishing techniques used by pros at Smith Mountain Lake. Learn how to catch stripers with live bait, trolling, jigging & topwater methods.
content:
Striped Bass Fishing Techniques That Actually Work

Want to know how to catch stripers consistently at Smith Mountain Lake? It starts with understanding the proven striped bass fishing techniques that Captain Tommy Moore uses every day on the water. These aren't internet theories or magazine fluff — they're battle-tested methods refined over years of professional guiding on one of Virginia's premier striper fisheries.

Here's your insider's guide to the techniques that put fish in the boat, courtesy of SML Wicked Striper Guide Service in Moneta, VA.

Downlining Live Bait

If there's one technique that defines striper fishing at Smith Mountain Lake, it's downlining. This method involves lowering live bait — typically gizzard shad or herring — on a weighted line to the exact depth where stripers are holding. Captain Tommy uses his Humminbird electronics to locate fish schools and determine their precise depth, then presents bait right in their face.

The beauty of downlining is its precision. Rather than hoping fish find your bait, you're delivering it directly to where they live. On a deep reservoir like Smith Mountain Lake where stripers can be suspended anywhere from 20 to 60 feet deep, that precision is the difference between a bent rod and an empty cooler.

Key elements of successful downlining:

  • Fresh, lively bait — Sluggish bait catches sluggish results. Captain Tommy nets fresh shad before every trip to ensure maximum liveliness and natural presentation.
  • Precise depth control — A 3-foot difference in presentation depth can mean the difference between hookups and silence. Captain Tommy uses line counters and marked lines to maintain exact depths.
  • Proper hook placement — Through the back or nose, depending on current and presentation style. Each has advantages in different situations.
  • Line management — Keeping lines tangle-free when multiple rods are deployed requires boat positioning and rod placement expertise.
  • Weight selection — Matching sinker weight to current, depth, and drift speed keeps bait in the zone naturally.

Free-Lining Live Bait

When stripers are feeding aggressively near the surface or in shallow water, free-lining is deadly effective. This technique uses little to no weight, allowing live bait to swim naturally and cover water on its own. It's especially effective during spring when fish are in tributary arms chasing bait, and during early-morning surface feeds in summer when stripers push shad to the top.

Free-lining requires a different mindset than downlining. You're giving the bait freedom to move, which means you need to read subtle line movements and tension changes to detect strikes. Captain Tommy coaches his clients on what to feel for — the sudden acceleration, the heavy pull, the line moving sideways — so they don't miss the moment.

Captain Tommy often pairs free-lining with downlining, running some rods deep and some near the surface to cover the entire water column simultaneously. This dual approach maximizes the chances of connecting with fish regardless of where they're feeding.

Trolling

Trolling is an excellent search technique for covering water and locating scattered fish across Smith Mountain Lake's vast open-water areas. Using umbrella rigs, diving plugs, or spoons pulled behind the boat at controlled speeds, trolling lets you present baits across a wide area efficiently. It's particularly effective in fall when stripers are roaming unpredictably and in winter when they're following bait schools along main-lake channels.

Captain Tommy uses planer boards to spread his trolling spread wider, covering more water and presenting baits at different distances from the boat to avoid spooking wary fish in clear water. He also varies lure depths using different weights, leader lengths, and diving depths to target multiple levels of the water column at once.

Vertical Jigging

When stripers are stacked tight on deep structure — a submerged hump, rock pile, or channel ledge — vertical jigging can be incredibly effective. Drop a heavy jig (1 to 3 ounces) directly below the boat, let it fall to the fish's level, then work it with sharp upward snaps and controlled falls.

This technique shines in winter when fish are lethargic and holding tight to structure in deep water. The erratic action of a jigged bait triggers reaction strikes from fish that might completely ignore a slow-moving live bait presentation. It's also excellent when live bait is scarce or when you want to target the biggest fish in a school, since larger jigs tend to attract larger predators.

Topwater Fishing

Nothing in freshwater fishing compares to the adrenaline rush of a 30-pound striper crushing a topwater plug on the surface. It's violent, explosive, and unforgettable. Surface fishing at Smith Mountain Lake happens primarily during low-light conditions — dawn, dusk, and overcast days. When stripers herd bait to the surface, the resulting feeding frenzy is visible from hundreds of yards away as water erupts with splashes and explosions.

Large walking baits, poppers, and prop baits all produce during these blitzes, but timing and boat positioning are critical. You need to be in position before the blitz starts, approach quietly, and cast accurately. Captain Tommy reads bird activity, surface disturbances, and his electronics to get you in position ahead of the action — giving you the best possible shot at casting into a surface feed.

Matching Technique to Conditions

The real skill in striper fishing isn't knowing these techniques individually — it's knowing when to deploy each one. Captain Tommy reads water temperature, clarity, weather patterns, time of year, moon phase, bait activity, and real-time fish behavior to select the right approach for each moment. Some days it's all downlining from start to finish. Other days, it's a dynamic combination of three or four techniques as conditions evolve throughout the trip.

That adaptability is what separates a professional fishing guide from a recreational angler with a boat. And it's why Captain Tommy's clients catch fish consistently — regardless of conditions.

title: Family Fishing Trips Smith Mountain Lake | Kids Welcome
metaDescription: Plan the perfect family fishing trip at Smith Mountain Lake. Kid-friendly fishing charters with Captain Tommy Moore. Safe, fun & unforgettable for all ages.
content:
Family Fishing Trips at Smith Mountain Lake

Looking for the ultimate family fishing trip at Smith Mountain Lake? Captain Tommy Moore and SML Wicked Striper Guide Service specialize in creating safe, fun, and memorable days on the water for families of all ages. Whether your kids have never held a rod or your teenagers are ready to battle a trophy striper, our kids fishing charter trips are designed with families in mind from start to finish.

Based in Moneta, VA, we're proud to introduce the next generation of anglers to the incredible fishing that Smith Mountain Lake offers — right here in Virginia's beautiful Blue Ridge Mountains.

Why Smith Mountain Lake Is Perfect for Family Fishing

Smith Mountain Lake isn't just a great striper fishery — it's a fantastic family destination. The lake is surrounded by the scenic Blue Ridge Mountains, the water is clean and clear, and the atmosphere is relaxed and welcoming. Unlike saltwater charters where rough seas and long boat runs can make kids miserable, Smith Mountain Lake is a calm, protected freshwater lake with short runs to fishing spots.

That means:

  • No seasickness worries — the lake surface is gentle even on breezy days
  • Short distances to fishing areas — kids aren't waiting an hour to start fishing
  • Calm water conditions on most days, suitable for all ages
  • Scenic surroundings that everyone enjoys, even non-anglers
  • Restroom breaks are easy — marinas and facilities are never far away
  • Wildlife sightings — eagles, ospreys, herons, and deer are regular companions

Captain Tommy's Approach with Families

Captain Tommy has hosted hundreds of families on Smith Mountain Lake over the years, and he knows that a successful family trip looks very different from a hardcore fishing trip. His approach with families focuses on three things: safety, fun, and fish-catching — in that order.

Safety first — always. Every passenger gets a Coast Guard-approved life jacket that fits properly. Captain Tommy maintains his boat to the highest standards and follows all safety protocols without exception. He's attentive to weather conditions and always makes conservative decisions when families with young children are aboard. Parents can relax knowing their kids are in capable, experienced hands.

Keep it fun and engaging. Captain Tommy is remarkably patient with young anglers. He helps with casting, baiting hooks, unhooking fish, and taking photos — all with a positive, encouraging attitude. He keeps the energy upbeat and celebrates every catch, whether it's a 2-pound bass or a 20-pound striper. If kids get restless between bites, he mixes things up — maybe some casting practice in a quiet cove, exploring a creek arm, watching an eagle's nest, or trying a different fishing technique to re-engage their interest.

Catch fish — because that's why you came. Kids want to catch fish, and so do their parents. Captain Tommy targets species and spots that maximize action for the whole family. That might mean starting with some easy panfish or bass to build confidence and excitement before moving to stripers, or it might mean going straight to a hot striper bite if conditions are right and the kids are ready. He reads the group's energy and adjusts his approach accordingly throughout the trip.

What Age Is Appropriate?

Captain Tommy has taken kids as young as 4 years old on fishing trips at Smith Mountain Lake, and each age group brings its own rewards:

Ages 4-6: Half-day trips are recommended for this age group. Their attention span is naturally limited, and a 4-hour trip hits the sweet spot — enough time for plenty of fishing action and lasting memories without pushing into meltdown territory. Expect lots of help from Captain Tommy with every aspect of fishing.

Ages 7-12: Full-day trips work great for this age range, especially if they've shown interest in fishing before. Kids this age often surprise their parents with how focused and capable they are when a fish is on the line. This is the prime window for creating lifelong fishing memories.

Teenagers: They're often the most enthusiastic anglers on the boat. There's something about battling a big striper — feeling the raw power of a 20- or 30-pound fish trying to rip the rod from their hands — that connects with teenagers in a way that few other activities can. It gets them off their phones and into the present moment.

Making Memories That Last a Lifetime

Ask any adult angler about their earliest fishing memories, and their eyes light up. Family fishing trips create bonds that last a lifetime. The excitement of a child catching their first striper, the teamwork of netting a big fish together, the storytelling at dinner afterward — these are the moments that families cherish for decades.

Captain Tommy encourages families to take plenty of photos and helps pose fish shots with the beautiful Smith Mountain Lake scenery as a backdrop. These photos become treasured keepsakes that you'll look back on for years to come.

Everything Is Included — Just Show Up

Family trips with SML Wicked Striper Guide Service include all rods, reels, tackle, live bait, and safety equipment. You don't need to own a single piece of fishing gear. Just bring sunscreen, snacks, drinks, and excitement. Captain Tommy handles absolutely everything else so you can focus on enjoying time with your family.

title: Smith Mountain Lake Fishing Report | What's Biting SML
metaDescription: Get the latest Smith Mountain Lake fishing report. Find out what's biting at SML, current striper patterns, water temps & hot techniques from Captain Tommy.
content:
Smith Mountain Lake Fishing Report: Current Conditions

Welcome to the latest Smith Mountain Lake fishing report from Captain Tommy Moore and SML Wicked Striper Guide Service. If you're wondering what's biting at SML, you've come to the right place. Captain Tommy is on the water multiple days per week out of Moneta, VA, and he's got the real-time intel you need to plan your next trip to Virginia's Blue Ridge Mountain striper fishery.

This report covers current conditions, active patterns, what's been working lately, and what to expect in the coming weeks on Smith Mountain Lake.

Current Water Conditions

Smith Mountain Lake's water conditions change throughout the year, and staying on top of them is key to consistent fishing success. Here's what we monitor and why it matters:

  • Water Temperature: The single most important factor in determining where stripers will be. Captain Tommy monitors surface and subsurface temps daily using his onboard electronics. Temperature dictates whether fish are shallow, deep, or somewhere in between.
  • Water Clarity: Smith Mountain Lake typically runs clear to moderately clear, with some stain after heavy rain events. Clear water means fish can be leader-shy and boat-wary, which affects presentation approach. Stained water often triggers more aggressive feeding.
  • Lake Level: Managed by Appalachian Power, levels fluctuate seasonally but generally remain within a predictable range. Rising water can activate shallow-water patterns, while dropping water pushes fish toward deeper structure.
  • Current/Flow: Generation schedules from Leesville Dam create current flow through the lower lake that significantly influences bait positioning and striper feeding behavior. Captain Tommy tracks generation schedules and plans trips around optimal flow conditions.
  • Thermocline Depth: During summer, the thermocline is the magic zone. Fish stack at this boundary where warm surface water meets cooler deep water. Knowing its exact depth is critical for presentation accuracy.

Striper Report

Striped bass remain the main attraction at Smith Mountain Lake, and the fishery continues to deliver outstanding results. Virginia's Department of Wildlife Resources maintains an active stocking program that supplements natural reproduction, resulting in a healthy mix of slot fish, keepers, and legitimate trophies swimming in these waters.

Current striper patterns shift with the seasons, but Captain Tommy stays on top of them through daily time on the water. His recent trips have been producing consistent action using live bait presentations targeted at the depths where fish are actively holding and feeding. Catch rates have been strong, with good numbers of quality fish mixed in with the occasional standout trophy that makes the day unforgettable.

When you book a trip, you're getting the most current fishing intelligence available — not a report from last week or a guess based on last year's patterns, but what's actually happening right now on the water. That real-time knowledge is the biggest advantage of fishing with a full-time guide.

Bait Situation

The health of the baitfish population directly affects striper fishing quality, and Smith Mountain Lake's forage base remains robust. The lake supports strong populations of gizzard shad, blueback herring, and alewife — the primary forage species that stripers depend on. Healthy bait populations mean healthy, well-fed stripers that fight hard and grow big.

Captain Tommy nets fresh bait before each trip using a cast net, which makes a substantial difference in catch rates compared to using day-old or store-bought bait. Fresh, lively bait that swims naturally and emits the right scent triggers significantly more strikes than tired, listless baitfish. It's a detail that matters enormously, and it's one of the many edges that a professional guide provides.

Other Species Report

While stripers are our primary target, Smith Mountain Lake also offers excellent fishing for multiple other species:

  • Largemouth Bass: Healthy population with fish up to 8+ pounds. Outstanding structure fishing along docks, laydowns, rocky banks, and submerged brush throughout the lake.
  • Smallmouth Bass: Found in the clearer, rockier sections of the lake. These bronze fighters routinely reach 4-5 pounds and are some of the hardest-fighting freshwater fish pound for pound.
  • Blue Catfish: Increasingly common in SML, with some real giants caught in recent years. An emerging fishery that's gaining attention from catfish anglers across the region.
  • Crappie: Excellent crappie fishing, especially in spring around brush piles and standing timber. Great for family trips and anglers who enjoy light-tackle action.
  • Walleye: A small but growing population provides occasional surprise catches that delight anglers.

What to Expect in the Coming Weeks

As seasons transition, so do the patterns on Smith Mountain Lake. Captain Tommy watches water temperature trends, bait movements, weather forecasts, and generation schedules to anticipate where fishing will be best in the coming weeks. This forward-looking approach means he's often ahead of the curve, finding fish and dialing in patterns before other anglers even realize things have changed.

Booking a trip 1-2 weeks out is ideal — it gives Captain Tommy time to incorporate the latest patterns and plan the optimal approach for your specific dates.

How to Use This Report

This fishing report gives you a valuable general picture, but conditions on Smith Mountain Lake can change quickly with rain events, temperature swings, and generation schedule changes. The best way to get a truly current report is to call Captain Tommy directly at (434) 610-3255. He'll give you an honest, no-hype assessment of current conditions and help you choose the best time to come.

Better yet, let him show you in person. Book your fishing trip today and experience Smith Mountain Lake's incredible striper fishing firsthand.

title: Virginia Fishing License Guide | VA DWR Requirements
metaDescription: Everything you need to know about Virginia fishing licenses for Smith Mountain Lake. VA DWR requirements, costs, exemptions & how to buy your license online.
content:
Virginia Fishing License: What You Need for Smith Mountain Lake

Planning a fishing trip to Smith Mountain Lake? Before you hit the water, you'll need to understand Virginia fishing license requirements. This comprehensive guide covers everything you need to know about VA DWR (Department of Wildlife Resources) licensing, so you can fish legally and focus on catching stripers with Captain Tommy Moore.

Here's the straightforward breakdown from SML Wicked Striper Guide Service in Moneta, VA — everything in one place so you don't have to search multiple websites.

Do I Need a Fishing License at Smith Mountain Lake?

Yes. Virginia law requires anyone 16 years of age or older to possess a valid fishing license when fishing in the Commonwealth's public waters, including Smith Mountain Lake. This applies to both Virginia residents and non-residents, whether you're fishing from a boat, dock, pier, or shoreline. It applies regardless of whether you're fishing with a guide or on your own.

The good news is that purchasing a license is quick, easy, and affordable. Don't let this small administrative step stop you from booking the fishing trip of a lifetime.

Types of Virginia Fishing Licenses

The Virginia DWR offers several license types to fit different needs and budgets:

  • Resident Freshwater Fishing License: For Virginia residents fishing in freshwater. Available as an annual license — the best value for Virginians who plan to fish multiple times throughout the year.
  • Non-Resident Freshwater Fishing License: For out-of-state anglers visiting Smith Mountain Lake. Available as annual, short-term, or daily licenses depending on how long your trip is.
  • 1-Day Trip License: Perfect for visitors who are only fishing one day — an economical choice for a single guided charter trip. No commitment beyond your fishing day.
  • 5-Day Trip License: Great for vacation anglers planning multiple days on the water at Smith Mountain Lake. Covers you for nearly a week of fishing at a fraction of the annual license cost.
  • Annual License: Best value if you plan to fish Virginia waters multiple times per year. One purchase covers you from July 1 through June 30.
  • Lifetime License: Virginia offers lifetime fishing licenses for residents — a one-time purchase that covers you forever.

Who Is Exempt from Licensing?

Virginia provides fishing license exemptions for several groups:

  • Children under 16 years old — no license required at all
  • Virginia residents 65 years or older (free license — but you still need to register with DWR)
  • Active-duty military personnel stationed in Virginia
  • Disabled veterans meeting specific criteria
  • Licensed Virginia landowners fishing on their own property or property they rent
  • Participants on designated free fishing days (Virginia hosts several each year, typically in early June)

If your kids are under 16, they can fish on Captain Tommy's boat without a license — one less thing to worry about when planning your family fishing trip. This makes Smith Mountain Lake an even more attractive destination for families.

How to Buy Your Virginia Fishing License

Purchasing a Virginia fishing license is straightforward and can be done several ways:

  • Online: Visit the Virginia DWR website (gooutdoorsvirginia.com) and purchase your license instantly. You can print it out or save the digital version on your phone. This is the fastest and most convenient option — it takes about 5 minutes.
  • By Phone: Call the DWR license sales line for phone-based purchases if you prefer speaking to a person.
  • In Person: Many sporting goods stores, bait shops, and Walmart locations in the Smith Mountain Lake area sell Virginia fishing licenses. If you're already in the Moneta, VA area, you can pick one up locally.
  • Mobile App: The Go Outdoors Virginia app allows you to purchase and carry your license digitally on your smartphone.

Captain Tommy strongly recommends buying your license online before your trip. Getting it done the night before eliminates any morning-of scrambling and ensures you're ready to fish the moment you step on the boat.

Striped Bass Regulations at Smith Mountain Lake

In addition to your fishing license, you'll need to know the current regulations for striped bass at Smith Mountain Lake. Virginia DWR sets specific rules that can change annually, including:

  • Minimum size limits — fish must meet a minimum length to be harvested
  • Daily creel (bag) limits — the maximum number of fish you can keep per day
  • Slot limits (when applicable) — specific size ranges that must be released
  • Seasonal restrictions — certain periods may have modified regulations
  • Gear restrictions — rules about hook types, numbers of rods, and methods

These regulations exist to protect and sustain Smith Mountain Lake's outstanding striper fishery for future generations. Captain Tommy stays up-to-date on all current regulations and ensures every single trip is fully compliant. He'll explain the current rules at the start of your trip so you know exactly what you can keep and what should be released.

Catch and Release Best Practices

Many anglers choose to practice catch and release at Smith Mountain Lake, especially with trophy-size fish. Proper release technique is important for fish survival — Captain Tommy will show you how to handle fish gently, minimize time out of water for photos, and support the fish horizontally to protect its internal organs before a clean release.

Don't Let Licensing Stress You Out

The licensing process is simple and quick. Captain Tommy is happy to answer any questions about licenses or regulations when you book your trip. He'll remind you to get your license squared away beforehand and can point you to the right resources if you need help.

Got your license? Great — now book your Smith Mountain Lake fishing trip with Captain Tommy Moore. Call (434) 610-3255 to reserve your date and get on the water.

title: Beginner Striper Fishing | Your First Charter Guide
metaDescription: New to striper fishing? This beginner's guide covers everything for your first fishing charter at Smith Mountain Lake. Tips, what to expect & how to prepare.
content:
Beginner Striper Fishing: Your First Charter at Smith Mountain Lake

Never been beginner striper fishing before? You're in for an incredible experience. Booking your first fishing charter at Smith Mountain Lake with Captain Tommy Moore is the perfect way to get started — absolutely no experience necessary. SML Wicked Striper Guide Service in Moneta, VA is built for anglers of all skill levels, and beginners are always genuinely welcome aboard.

Here's everything you need to know to make your first striper fishing trip a great one — from what to expect to how to prepare.

What Is Striper Fishing?

Striped bass (also called stripers, rockfish, or linesiders) are powerful, hard-fighting freshwater game fish that can grow to 50+ pounds in Smith Mountain Lake. They're the apex predator of the lake — fast, strong, and genuinely challenging to catch consistently without the right knowledge, equipment, and technique. That's exactly why a guided charter with an experienced captain is the best way for beginners to start.

Unlike bass fishing where you might cast to visible shoreline cover, striper fishing at Smith Mountain Lake is more technical. Fish are often suspended in open water at specific depths that vary with season, weather, and time of day. Locating them requires advanced electronics, experience reading sonar, and quality live bait. Captain Tommy handles all of that complexity — your job is to hold on tight when a big striper hits your line.

Do I Need Any Experience?

Absolutely not. Captain Tommy has introduced hundreds of first-time anglers to striper fishing over his guiding career, and he's incredibly patient and thorough with beginners. He doesn't assume you know anything, and he doesn't make you feel bad for asking questions. Before lines go in the water, he'll walk you through everything you need to know:

  • How to hold and operate the rods and reels — proper grip, drag adjustment, casting basics
  • How to detect a bite and when to set the hook — the feel and timing that separates a hookup from a miss
  • Proper fish-fighting technique — letting the rod and reel do the work instead of muscling the fish
  • How to safely handle fish for photos and release — protecting both you and the fish
  • Basic boat safety and etiquette — where to stand, how to move, what to avoid
  • How to read the fish-finding electronics — so you understand what's happening beneath the surface

You don't need to know a single thing going in. You'll know plenty coming out. Many of Captain Tommy's beginner clients become passionate anglers after just one trip.

What Does a First Charter Look Like?

Here's a typical timeline for a half-day trip, so you know exactly what to expect:

  • Pre-trip (evening before): Captain Tommy confirms meeting time and location via text or phone call. He'll also give you a quick weather update and any last-minute tips for preparation.
  • Arrival: Meet Captain Tommy at the dock near Moneta, VA. He'll help you aboard, show you around the boat, and give a thorough safety briefing. This is relaxed and friendly — not a military inspection.
  • Bait run: Captain Tommy may net fresh shad with a cast net before heading to fishing spots. This is actually interesting to watch and gives you a sense of how the day's fishing strategy comes together.
  • Fishing (3-4 hours): Active fishing with hands-on instruction every step of the way. Captain Tommy positions you over fish, rigs your bait, puts the rod in your hands, and coaches you through every bite and fight.
  • Photos and wrap-up: Photos with your catch against the beautiful Smith Mountain Lake backdrop, tips for your next trip, and a relaxed ride back to the dock.

Tips for Your First Striper Charter

Captain Tommy wants your first trip to be amazing. Here are his top tips for beginners heading out on Smith Mountain Lake:

Dress in layers. Mornings on the lake can be surprisingly cool, even in summer months. The Blue Ridge Mountains create temperature swings that catch visitors off guard. You can always peel off a layer as the day warms up, but you can't add what you didn't bring.

Wear non-marking, non-slip shoes. Tennis shoes or deck shoes are perfect. Avoid flip-flops, sandals, or bare feet — the boat deck can be slippery when wet, and a secure footing is essential for safety and fighting fish.

Bring sunscreen and polarized sunglasses. Reflection off Smith Mountain Lake's clear water amplifies UV exposure significantly. Apply sunscreen before you arrive. Polarized sunglasses aren't just comfortable — they help you see fish activity and bait movement in the water, adding another dimension to the experience.

Eat a good breakfast. Don't show up on an empty stomach. A solid breakfast gives you energy for an active morning on the water and helps prevent any queasy feelings if you're sensitive to boat motion.

Ask questions — lots of them. Captain Tommy genuinely loves teaching. Don't hesitate to ask why he's doing something, what the electronics are showing, how a technique works, or anything else that crosses your mind. The more you ask, the more you learn, and the more engaged you'll be throughout the trip.

Don't stress about doing it wrong. Everyone has to start somewhere. Captain Tommy has seen it all — missed hooksets, tangled lines, dropped fish. It's all part of the learning process, and he handles every situation with patience and good humor.

Will I Actually Catch Fish?

While fishing is never 100% guaranteed (they don't call it "catching" for a reason), Captain Tommy's success rate with beginners is excellent. His live bait techniques are naturally forgiving for new anglers — when a hungry striper eats a live shad, the hookup is often automatic and hard to miss. And because Captain Tommy puts you directly on actively feeding fish using his electronics and experience, your odds are dramatically better than trying to figure it out alone.

Most first-time clients leave Smith Mountain Lake with multiple fish catches under their belt, a newfound appreciation for the sport, sore arms from fighting stripers, and plans already forming for their next trip.

Ready for your first striper fishing adventure? Book your charter with Captain Tommy Moore today or call (434) 610-3255. No experience needed — just enthusiasm and a willingness to have fun.

title: Smith Mountain Lake Best Fishing Spots & Hidden Gems
metaDescription: Discover the best fishing spots on Smith Mountain Lake. Captain Tommy Moore reveals SML hidden gems, top striper areas & where trophy fish hold year-round.
content:
Smith Mountain Lake Best Fishing Spots and Hidden Gems

Every angler wants to know the Smith Mountain Lake best fishing spots, and Captain Tommy Moore knows them better than almost anyone on the water. After years of professional guiding on SML, he's catalogued an extensive library of productive structure, holding areas, and SML hidden gems that consistently produce striped bass throughout the year — spots that don't show up in online forums or magazine articles.

While a guide never gives away all his secrets, here's a detailed look at the types of spots that make Smith Mountain Lake such an incredible striper fishery — and why fishing them with a local expert from Moneta, VA makes all the difference in the world.

Main-Lake Humps and Ridges

Smith Mountain Lake has numerous underwater humps and ridges that are completely invisible from the surface but light up like Christmas trees on sonar. These submerged structures rise from deep water and create current breaks, ambush points, and baitfish congregation areas that stripers can't resist.

The best humps top out in the 20- to 35-foot range with deep water (60+ feet) surrounding them on multiple sides. Baitfish congregate around these structures because the rising bottom creates upwelling currents that concentrate plankton and microorganisms. Stripers stack up below the bait schools, feeding opportunistically on anything that drops out of the main school. Captain Tommy has GPS waypoints for dozens of these structures and knows which ones produce during specific seasons, weather conditions, and times of day.

What makes these spots true hidden gems is that many of them are miles from the nearest visible shoreline feature. You'd never find them without detailed sonar and years of exploration. Even anglers with good electronics often motor right over these goldmines without realizing what's beneath them.

Creek Arm Mouths and Transition Zones

Where creek arms meet the main lake body, there's usually a subtle depth transition and current seam that concentrates bait and predator fish. These junction points are especially productive in spring (when stripers move into tributaries for the spawn) and fall (when cooling water pushes bait back toward the main lake). The mixing of creek water with main-lake water creates temperature and oxygen differentials that fish use as feeding triggers.

Smith Mountain Lake has numerous creek arms — the Roanoke River channel, Blackwater River arm, and dozens of smaller tributaries. Each one creates a unique feeding opportunity with its own personality. Some produce best on rising water, others on falling water. Some fire during morning hours, others in the afternoon. Captain Tommy has decoded these patterns through years of meticulous observation and record-keeping.

Channel Bends and Ledges

The old Roanoke River channel winds through Smith Mountain Lake like an underwater highway, and every bend in that channel creates a ledge where the bottom drops from shallow to deep. Stripers patrol these ledges constantly, using the depth change for cover and ambush. The outside bend of each channel turn creates the steepest drop-off — and that's where the biggest fish hold.

Winter fishing on these channel ledges can be exceptional, with fish stacked in tight schools along the steepest breaks. Captain Tommy's electronics reveal these concentrations clearly, and his live bait presentations delivered right to the fish's depth trigger consistent strikes even on the coldest days.

Bridge Pilings and Man-Made Structure

Smith Mountain Lake has several bridges crossing its arms, and the concrete pilings create artificial reef-like structure that attracts bait and game fish year-round. The shade, current break, and vertical surfaces these pilings provide make them reliable holding spots for stripers of all sizes. The bridge at the Route 122 crossing is particularly noteworthy, but Captain Tommy knows numerous other man-made structure points that produce consistently.

Deep-Water Suspending Areas

Some of the best striper fishing on Smith Mountain Lake happens over what appears to be nothing — open water with no visible structure, no shoreline features, and no obvious reason for fish to be there. But beneath the surface, Captain Tommy's electronics reveal massive schools of bait suspended over deep main-lake channels, with predatory stripers lurking below.

These open-water bites require precise boat positioning, accurate depth control, and the ability to drift or slow-troll through the zone without spooking fish. They also require the confidence to stay on unmarked open water when the fishing feels like it should happen closer to shore. That confidence comes from experience — experience that Captain Tommy has in abundance.

The True Hidden Gems

Beyond the well-known structural categories, Smith Mountain Lake harbors countless true hidden gems that only guides and locals who spend serious time on the water ever discover. A submerged rock pile that doesn't appear on any map. An old road bed from before the lake was impounded. A single standing tree in 40 feet of water that holds fish like a magnet. A subtle bottom composition change that concentrates bait in a seemingly featureless area.

These are the spots that don't appear in fishing forums, YouTube videos, or magazine articles. They're discovered through years of exploring, marking waypoints, returning to check and recheck, and recognizing patterns that only reveal themselves over hundreds of trips. Captain Tommy's waypoint library is one of his most valuable professional assets — each point represents a location he's verified through catching fish, not just theory.

Why Spots Alone Aren't Enough

Here's the honest truth: even if you had every one of Captain Tommy's waypoints programmed into your GPS, you still wouldn't fish them as effectively as he does. Knowing the spot is only half the equation. You also need to know:

  • What time of year each spot produces best — and when to avoid it
  • What depth to present bait at each location — it changes with seasons and conditions
  • How weather fronts, wind direction, and water conditions affect each spot differently
  • Which direction to approach without spooking fish off the structure
  • When to commit and wait versus when to pull up and move to the next option
  • How current from dam generation affects each spot's productivity

That's the real value of fishing with Captain Tommy Moore. He pairs his exhaustive spot knowledge with real-time decision-making that maximizes every minute of your time on productive water at Smith Mountain Lake.

Book your trip today and fish Smith Mountain Lake's best spots — including the hidden gems — with the guide who knows them best. Call (434) 610-3255.

title: What to Bring on a Fishing Charter | Packing List
metaDescription: Complete fishing trip packing list for your Smith Mountain Lake charter. What to bring, what to wear & what Captain Tommy Moore provides on every guided trip.
content:
What to Bring on Your Fishing Charter at Smith Mountain Lake

Booked your trip with Captain Tommy Moore? Excellent — now let's make sure you're fully prepared. Knowing what to bring on a fishing charter makes the difference between a comfortable, enjoyable day on Smith Mountain Lake and a miserable one. This comprehensive fishing trip packing list covers everything you need (and don't need) for your guided trip with SML Wicked Striper Guide Service out of Moneta, VA.

What Captain Tommy Provides

First, let's cover what you DON'T need to bring because it's already included with every charter:

  • All fishing rods, reels, and tackle — professional grade, properly maintained
  • Fresh live bait (gizzard shad and herring) — netted the morning of your trip
  • Coast Guard-approved life jackets for every passenger
  • State-of-the-art fish-finding electronics and navigation equipment
  • Cooler with ice for keeping your catch fresh
  • All terminal tackle — hooks, weights, swivels, leaders, everything

Leave your fishing gear at home unless you have a sentimental attachment to a particular rod. Captain Tommy's equipment is professional-grade, properly maintained, and matched to the type of fishing you'll be doing on Smith Mountain Lake. His tackle is always rigged and ready — you won't waste precious fishing time tying knots or searching for the right hook size.

Essential Items to Bring

Here's your must-have packing list, organized by category:

Sun Protection (Critical — Don't Skip This)

  • Sunscreen (SPF 30+ minimum, waterproof formula) — reapply every 2 hours on the water
  • Polarized sunglasses (protects your eyes AND helps you see fish activity in the water)
  • Hat with a brim (baseball cap or wide-brim hat — your choice)
  • Lightweight long-sleeve shirt or sun hoodie (UPF-rated fabric is ideal)
  • Lip balm with SPF — often forgotten but important on the water

Sun exposure on Smith Mountain Lake is serious. The water reflects UV rays from below while the sun hits you from above, creating a double-exposure effect. Even on overcast days, you can burn badly without protection. Take this category seriously — sunburn can ruin the second half of a full-day trip.

Clothing — Dress for Success

  • Dress in layers — mornings on Smith Mountain Lake can be cool, even in summer, thanks to the Blue Ridge Mountain elevation
  • Rain jacket (lightweight, packable — Virginia weather changes fast in the mountains)
  • Non-marking, non-slip shoes (tennis shoes, deck shoes, or rubber-soled boots)
  • In cold months: warm thermal layers, waterproof outer shell, waterproof gloves, and a warm beanie or watch cap
  • A change of shirt in your car — you may get splashed or sweaty

Food and Drinks — Fuel for Fishing

  • Water bottles — stay hydrated, especially in summer. Bring more than you think you'll need.
  • Snacks (granola bars, sandwiches, fruit, trail mix, crackers and cheese)
  • Coffee or your preferred morning beverage in an insulated mug
  • A small soft cooler for your food and drinks — hard coolers take up too much deck space
  • Avoid messy or strong-smelling foods — they attract insects and can make the boat unpleasant

Other Essentials

  • Valid Virginia fishing license (buy online beforehand — see our licensing guide)
  • Camera or fully charged smartphone for photos and video
  • Any personal medications you may need during the trip
  • Motion sickness medication if you're prone (though Smith Mountain Lake is calm, some people are extra sensitive)
  • A ziplock bag for your phone, wallet, and car keys
  • Hand towel or small rag — useful throughout the day

What NOT to Bring

Just as important as what to bring is what to leave behind:

  • Hard-soled shoes or boots with black soles — they mark up the boat deck permanently
  • Glass bottles — breakage hazard on a rocking boat. Use cans or plastic containers.
  • Excessive gear or luggage — the boat has limited storage, so pack light and efficient
  • Heavy cologne or perfume — fish can detect scents transferred through the water from your hands
  • Bananas — yes, it's a real fishing superstition that goes back centuries, and Captain Tommy respects the tradition. Leave the bananas onshore.
  • Expensive jewelry — rings, watches, and bracelets can catch on lines and equipment. Leave the bling at home.
  • Negative attitudes — fishing is supposed to be fun, even when the fish are being stubborn

Tips by Season at Smith Mountain Lake

Spring (March-May): Mornings are chilly but afternoons warm up nicely. Layers are absolutely critical during this transitional season. Bring a light rain jacket — spring showers are common and often arrive quickly in the Blue Ridge Mountains.

Summer (June-August): Heat and sun are your main adversaries. Prioritize sun protection, aggressive hydration, and lightweight, breathable clothing. Bring extra water — you'll drink more than you expect. A cooling towel is a nice luxury item.

Fall (September-November): Similar layering strategy to spring — the transition from warm to cool can happen fast. Morning fog is common and burns off beautifully. The stunning fall foliage surrounding Smith Mountain Lake makes for incredible photo backgrounds.

Winter (December-February): Dress warm — this is non-negotiable. Insulated, waterproof outer layers, thermal base layers, waterproof gloves, and a warm hat are essential. Chemical hand warmers are a smart addition. The fishing can be absolutely outstanding in winter, but comfort requires proper preparation.

Pro Tips from Captain Tommy

  • Apply sunscreen BEFORE you get to the dock — it needs 15-20 minutes to absorb into your skin for full protection
  • Bring a ziplock bag for your phone and wallet — one rogue wave or splash can ruin electronics
  • Eat a solid breakfast before the trip — fishing on an empty stomach is no fun
  • Charge your phone fully the night before — you'll want battery for photos all day
  • Arrive 10-15 minutes early to get settled, use the restroom, and start the day relaxed
  • Bring a sense of humor — even when things don't go perfectly, the day on the water is what matters

Ready to pack up and head out? Book your Smith Mountain Lake fishing charter with Captain Tommy Moore or call (434) 610-3255. Check our pricing page for trip options and rates.

title: Live Bait vs Lures for Stripers | Best Bait for SML
metaDescription: Live bait vs lures for striper fishing at Smith Mountain Lake. Captain Tommy Moore breaks down the best bait options, when to use each & why live bait wins.
content:
Live Bait vs Lures for Stripers at Smith Mountain Lake

The live bait vs lures debate has raged among striper anglers for decades, and at Smith Mountain Lake, both methods have their place in a serious angler's arsenal. But when it comes to consistently putting trophy-class striped bass in the boat day after day, Captain Tommy Moore has a clear favorite backed by years of guiding data. Here's his honest, experience-based breakdown of the best bait for Smith Mountain Lake striper fishing.

Why Live Bait Dominates at Smith Mountain Lake

Captain Tommy uses live bait as his primary presentation on the vast majority of guided trips, and for good reason — it outproduces artificial lures by a significant margin in most situations on SML. Here's the detailed reasoning:

Natural presentation that can't be replicated. A live gizzard shad swimming at 30 feet looks, smells, moves, vibrates, and behaves exactly like what stripers eat every single day of their lives. No lure in existence — regardless of price or engineering — can perfectly replicate all of those sensory triggers simultaneously. Smith Mountain Lake stripers see plenty of artificial baits throughout the year, especially in high-traffic areas near marinas and popular points. Live bait doesn't trigger those learned avoidance behaviors that develop in pressured fish.

Scent trail that draws fish from distance. Live bait emits natural scent, oils, and amino acids that attract stripers from significant distances in the water column. In Smith Mountain Lake's characteristically clear water, visual appeal certainly matters — but scent is often what triggers the final commitment to strike. Wounded or stressed baitfish release additional chemical signals that stripers have evolved to detect and respond to aggressively.

Foolproof for all skill levels. A live shad does the work for you. It swims, it struggles, it attracts attention through natural movement and vibration. For beginners on their first charter, live bait fishing is far more productive because the bait does the heavy lifting. There's no retrieve cadence to master, no rod action to perfect, no color selection to agonize over. You hold the rod, the bait does its job, and the striper does the rest.

Captain Tommy's Live Bait Arsenal

The go-to live baits at Smith Mountain Lake include:

  • Gizzard Shad: The bread and butter of SML striper fishing. These thick-bodied baitfish are the primary natural forage for stripers in the lake, and they're hardy on the hook — staying lively for extended periods. Captain Tommy nets fresh shad before every single trip using a cast net, selecting the right size for the conditions.
  • Blueback Herring: Smaller and more active than gizzard shad, herring are excellent when stripers are keyed on smaller forage. Their frantic, erratic swimming action drives stripers wild and can trigger strikes when shad presentations are getting ignored.
  • Alewife: Another natural forage species in SML that stripers encounter and feed on regularly. Similar in profile to herring but with subtle size and movement differences that can make a difference on certain days when fish are being selective.

Fresh bait is absolutely non-negotiable in Captain Tommy's approach. He won't fish with yesterday's leftover bait, period. The liveliness, natural swimming action, and scent output of freshly caught shad dramatically outperforms tired, listless baitfish that have been sitting in a bait tank overnight. This commitment to fresh bait is one of many details that separate a professional guide from a casual approach.

When Lures Have the Edge

That said, artificial lures absolutely have their place in striper fishing at Smith Mountain Lake, and Captain Tommy carries a full tackle arsenal for the situations where artificials outperform live bait:

Surface feeding frenzies. When stripers are blasting bait on the surface in an explosive blitz, there's no time to carefully rig live bait — you need to get a topwater plug or jerkbait into the action immediately. Large walking baits, poppers, and swim baits excel during these chaotic surface events. The speed of deployment and the ability to cast repeatedly into the action make artificials the clear winner during blitzes.

Covering water quickly. Trolling artificial lures (umbrella rigs, diving plugs, spoons) lets you search large areas of Smith Mountain Lake efficiently. This is particularly valuable when fish are scattered and you need to locate concentrations before committing to a specific area with live bait. Captain Tommy often uses trolling as a search tool, then switches to live bait once he's located active fish.

Tight structure fishing. When stripers are tucked against bridge pilings, rock walls, or other snag-heavy structure, a precisely placed jig or swimbait can access spots where live bait rigs with weights and hooks would immediately hang up and be lost.

Heavy current situations. During heavy generation from Leesville Dam, strong current can make live bait presentations difficult to control and keep in the zone. Heavy jigs and bucktails that maintain bottom contact in current produce well in these conditions, and their weight keeps them in the strike zone when lighter live bait rigs wash downstream too quickly.

Popular Artificial Lures for SML Stripers

  • Umbrella rigs (A-rigs): Simulate a school of fleeing baitfish — absolutely deadly for trolling open-water areas
  • Swimbaits (3-6 inch): Paddle-tail soft plastics on jig heads — versatile and effective in many situations
  • Bucktail jigs (1-3 oz): The classic striper lure for vertical jigging deep structure — simple and effective
  • Topwater plugs: Walking baits, poppers, and prop baits for explosive surface feeding situations
  • Crankbaits: Deep-diving plugs for trolling specific depth zones — allows precise depth targeting
  • Spoons: Flutter spoons for vertical presentations and trolling — great flash and vibration

The Verdict from Captain Tommy

For consistent striper catching at Smith Mountain Lake across all conditions and seasons, live bait wins decisively. Captain Tommy estimates that roughly 80% of the fish his clients catch each year come on live bait presentations. But the best anglers — and the best guides — know how to use both methods effectively and recognize the situations where artificials have the advantage.

On Captain Tommy's boat, you'll always experience the most productive approach for the day's specific conditions, whether that's live bait, artificial lures, or a strategic combination of both throughout the trip.

Want to see what's working right now on Smith Mountain Lake? Book your trip with Captain Tommy Moore or call (434) 610-3255. He'll put the right bait in front of the right fish — guaranteed effort, every time.

title: Night Fishing Smith Mountain Lake | Striper After Dark
metaDescription: Experience night fishing at Smith Mountain Lake for giant stripers. Captain Tommy Moore's guide to after-dark striper fishing tactics, safety & best seasons.
content:
Night Fishing Smith Mountain Lake: Stripers After Dark

Night fishing at Smith Mountain Lake is one of the best-kept secrets among serious striper anglers in Virginia. While most fishermen are back at the dock grilling their catch and swapping stories, the real trophy hunters know that striper night fishing can produce some of the biggest fish of the entire year. Captain Tommy Moore of SML Wicked Striper Guide Service in Moneta, VA shares his insights on this exciting and underutilized after-dark fishery.

Why Stripers Feed Aggressively at Night

Striped bass are crepuscular and nocturnal predators by nature — meaning they're biologically wired to hunt most effectively during low-light conditions and after full darkness falls. At Smith Mountain Lake, this natural predatory behavior is amplified by several environmental factors:

Reduced boat traffic and noise. During summer months, Smith Mountain Lake sees heavy recreational boat traffic throughout the day. Wakes, engine noise, water skiers, jet skis, and general human activity push stripers deeper and make them significantly more cautious and less willing to commit to baits. At night, the lake goes quiet and still. Fish move shallower, spread out, and feed with much greater aggression and confidence.

Baitfish vulnerability increases dramatically. Gizzard shad and herring are visually oriented prey fish that are less aware of predators in darkness. They drift into shallower water and congregate around lights, structure, and current areas where stripers can ambush them with devastating efficiency. The predator-prey dynamic shifts heavily in the striper's favor after dark.

Water temperature relief in summer. During the hottest months, surface temperatures at SML can exceed 85 degrees Fahrenheit. Stripers are cool-water fish that physiologically prefer temperatures in the 60s. At night, the upper water column cools slightly, and fish that were pinned in the thermocline during the day become more active and range further through the water column. This expanded activity zone creates more feeding opportunities and makes fish more accessible.

Reduced fishing pressure. Very few anglers fish Smith Mountain Lake after dark. This means the fish encounter far fewer presentations, making them less wary and more likely to eat without hesitation. It's simple math — less pressure equals more willing fish.

Best Seasons for Night Fishing SML

Night fishing at Smith Mountain Lake produces year-round for those willing to brave the conditions, but certain seasons stand out as particularly productive:

Late Spring (May-June): Post-spawn stripers are ravenous after the energy expenditure of spawning, and they actively feed at night to rebuild their reserves. The combination of warming water, lengthening days, and hungry fish creates outstanding after-dark action. This may be the most consistent window for night fishing success.

Summer (July-August): This is prime time for night fishing on Smith Mountain Lake, and the season when after-dark trips offer the biggest advantage over daytime fishing. Brutal daytime heat and heavy boat traffic push fish deep and make them reluctant during daylight hours. But at night, the lake transforms. Some of Captain Tommy's biggest fish each year — true trophies in the 30- to 40-pound class — come during summer night trips.

Early Fall (September-October): As days shorten and the bait base becomes more concentrated, night fishing remains highly productive. Cooling water temperatures make fish more aggressive overall, and the transition period between summer and fall patterns produces both impressive numbers and exceptional size.

Night Fishing Techniques That Produce

Fishing after dark at Smith Mountain Lake requires meaningful adjustments to standard daytime tactics:

  • Lighted areas as fish magnets: Dock lights, bridge lights, marina lights, and dedicated underwater fishing lights attract baitfish like moths to a flame, and stripers follow their food. Fishing near these light sources — particularly on the shadow edges where light transitions to dark — can be incredibly productive. Stripers use the dark side of the light boundary as an ambush lane.
  • Slow, deliberate presentations: After dark, fish hunt more by lateral line detection (vibration sensing) and scent than by sight. Slow-moving live bait or subtle, swimming lure presentations consistently outperform fast, reaction-style techniques that rely on visual triggers.
  • Shallow structure comes alive: Stripers move significantly shallower at night than they hold during the day. Points, humps, and flats that top out in 10-20 feet — structures that may be fishless during daylight — become productive nighttime feeding stations.
  • Sound discipline is critical: The lake is quiet at night, and fish become hyper-aware of unnatural sounds. Minimize talking, eliminate banging or dropping objects on the deck, and reduce unnecessary movement on the boat. The quieter you are, the closer fish will approach.
  • Moon phase matters: Full moon periods provide enough ambient light for stripers to feed visually in shallow water, often producing the most aggressive surface action. New moon periods favor structure-oriented fishing in darker conditions where fish rely more on vibration and scent.

Safety Considerations for Night Fishing

Night fishing adds a layer of complexity that requires experience, preparation, and strict safety protocols. Captain Tommy takes night fishing safety extremely seriously:

  • All required navigation lights are maintained in perfect working order and verified before departure
  • Every passenger wears a Coast Guard-approved life jacket — no exceptions at night
  • GPS and depth-finding electronics guide all navigation — no relying on visual landmarks in darkness
  • Multiple flashlights and headlamps with red-light modes are available to preserve night vision
  • Intimate familiarity with the lake eliminates navigation guesswork and hazard avoidance
  • Weather is monitored closely — night trips are cancelled without hesitation if conditions are questionable
  • A detailed float plan is filed so someone onshore knows the trip itinerary

The Night Fishing Experience

There's something genuinely magical about being on Smith Mountain Lake at night. The Blue Ridge Mountains form a dark, dramatic silhouette against a sky filled with stars that you can't see from the city. The water surface is glass-calm and mirror-smooth. Occasional splashes from feeding fish break the silence and quicken your pulse. The haunting call of a great horned owl echoes across the cove.

And then your rod loads up with the unmistakable, arm-wrenching weight of a big striper, and the fight begins in near-total darkness — guided only by the sound of drag screaming and the deep bend of the rod against the star-filled sky.

It's a sensory experience unlike any daytime fishing trip, and many of Captain Tommy's clients who try night fishing once become regular after-dark customers who request it specifically.

Book a Night Fishing Trip

Night fishing trips at Smith Mountain Lake are available seasonally based on conditions and demand. Contact Captain Tommy Moore to discuss availability and current night bite conditions. Book your trip online or call (434) 610-3255 to experience striper fishing after dark on one of Virginia's finest lakes.
